text:
Mentorship sessions were never just about resumes, interviews, or jobs for me.

Sometimes they turn into:
'Didi, I think I'm not good enough.'
'I've applied to 500 jobs.'
'My parents think I'm wasting my time.'
'I don't know what to do anymore.'

Behind every 'placed candidate' post,
there's usually:
• anxiety
• self-doubt
• burnout
• rejection
• comparison
• crying at 2 AM wondering if life will ever work out

A lot of people don't need just career guidance.

They need someone who believes in them
before they start believing in themselves again.

That's why mentorship matters.

Not because it magically gets you a job.
But because sometimes,
one conversation can stop someone from giving up on themselves.

And that changes everything.

If you are struggling to find a job, crack an offer, try this -

'If I had to start from zero in 2026…
no job, no experience, no connections…'
 
What would ACTUALLY work?
Not theory.
Not motivation.
Just execution.

How will I plan to land a job in 2026?

This Sunday, I'm showing exactly that.
– How to get your first proof of work
– How to stand out without referrals
– What actually gets replies (not ghosting)

Is it just me or LinkedIn reach is bad for everyone?

I mean what's the point of posting anymore? At this point, my impressions depend more on LinkedIn's mood swings than content quality.

Wohi ek chatpati photo daal do toh 1000 likes, 780 comments.

The platform that was meant for networking, educational and professional content is dying.

The least that LinkedIn can do is to tell people how the algo works, what changed, what gets reach, AI generated content reach etc.

Disappointed.

Webinar Alert – This Sunday meet me for a webinar on How will I restart my career if I had to start from Scratch.
 
If I had to start my career in 2026 – No job, No Experience, No Connections.
How will I apply differently?
- I won't be applying to 200 jobs daily.
- Buy 15 random courses.
- Moving without direction.

Because that's exactly why people stay stuck.

Right now, people are:
→ Learning everything
→ Finishing nothing
→ Staying stuck
Harsh, but true.

If I had to restart my career in 2026 from zero…I would ignore 90% of the advice on the internet.

And follow a simple, focused plan that actually leads to income in 3–6 months.
 
So, I will do things differently – let me teach you how.
Most people don't fail because they're not capable. They fail because they don't have a clear system.

So I'm doing a live webinar where I'll break this down step-by-step.

Not theory. Not motivation.
A practical roadmap to go from confused → earning. The SkillCat
📅 3rd May Sunday [less than 30 people]
⏰ 12 PM IST
💸 ₹49 (less than your coffee, but worth way more)
If you're feeling stuck, this might change things for you. Join me now!
 
Comment 'START' and I will send you the link.
(Please connect with me so I can send it in your inbox)

If you can help me, please share/repost with your friends.

I think the best suited Rishtedaar for Corporate is BUAs - I mean look at the traits -

They would not even survive, they will dominate.

1. They always know about the gossips (Kiski kitni salary hai, Kiska promotion hua, etc)

2. Keep track of everything: Who said what, Who did what, When it happened, Who was involved etc.
→ Internal audit ready

3. Politics? Say less.
They don't just play it.
They design it.

4. Memory = Database. 'Tumne 2014 mein June 30 ko 10 bje yeh bola tha'

5. Passive-aggressive communication GOATs
'Main toh bas keh rahi hoon…'
(But she's not just saying.)

Honestly…
If you've survived a few kalesh with BUAs, corporate shouldn't scare you.
You've already handled: pressure, judgment, comparison and unexpected questioning without prep.

Sometimes, one right decision can change everything.
Maybe this is yours.

Some people are not 'less capable.'

They just never got:
– the right manager
– the right timing
– the right environment

Talent grows in the right conditions.
Not under constant pressure.

Nobody cares if you're a Product Manager, Analyst, or VP.
Because if your friend is crying at 2 a.m.,
You're not a Senior Data Scientist.
You're a human.

If your mom is sick,
You don't run SQL queries.
You hold her hand.

Stop hiding behind big titles.
Be a good person first, then a talented, skillful professional.
Nobody puts your job title on your grave.

Using Sundays to feel more - as my headline says - A human first. A person who feels, not just hustles.

So, starting today, I will be dropping a weekly Sunday Episode - where we will be talking about emotions, feelings, complexities and so much more.

Today - let's talk about Healing and Grief.

Episode 1 - Healing and Grief.

Healing from a trauma. Healing from loss. Healing from anything.

People often think healing means: 'One day I'll enjoy things like before.'

But the truth is softer than that.

If you are someone who is going through a period of grief - you will agree to this. (If you aren't, ask anyone)

Grief doesn't just show up as sadness.

It sits quietly in the background… like a low hum you can't switch off.

You can be laughing, dancing, sitting with people you love - and still feel that small pull inside your chest.

Almost like - you're here but not fully here.

And the worst part?
You don't even know when it will hit.
A song.
A smell.
A random thought.

And suddenly you're back there again.
Trying to hold yourself together while the world thinks you're okay.

Here's the truth I'm learning:
Grief doesn't leave. It just changes its seat.

From your chest…to the back of your mind.

That doesn't mean that you have lost the ability to feel joy, it just means your heart holds two things at once - joy and loss.

After loss, you don't go back to how things were.
You slowly learn how to carry the pain differently.

At first, grief sits in front of everything.
Then it starts sitting beside things.
Slowly it sits in the background (The point is that it is there - it doesn't go away)

You maybe in a phase where Happiness has a shadow.

That's okay.

Just a reminder - slowly, very slowly -

there will be moments where you forget the pain for a few seconds longer than before.
Then minutes.
Then maybe an hour.

And one day, you'll notice:
'I enjoyed that… completely.'

Not because the grief disappeared -
but because your heart grew around it.

This is the reality.

A man died on the warehouse floor, systems didn't stop, machines did not stop, target did not stop.

A co-worker wanted to help but couldn't because of 'company policy'.

His colleagues were told not to look and get back to their work.

One employee said that the person who fell to the ground had extensive blood coming from the head.

It said that while the man lay dead for more than an hour, the work in the facility was going on as usual, as workers were instructed to continue fetching, picking, loading items and the management was deliberating what to do next.

A woman ran to help the man when he collapsed and started to give CPR. Another employee asked her supervisor whether she should give CPR. 'It has to be management or safety team. Please get back to work,' the supervisor reportedly said. 'Just turn around and not look. Let's get back to work,' the supervisor added.

People just stepped around him.
Not because they didn't care -
But because somewhere along the way,
we were trained not to.

'Let's get back to work.'

Read that again.

A human life ended… and productivity was still the priority.

This is what corporate culture looks like
when it forgets what it's built on - people.

We've normalized things that should shake us:
Working through pain
Ignoring burnout
Choosing deadlines over dignity

And the scariest part?
It doesn't happen in one day.
It happens slowly.

Are we building careers?
Or slowly becoming numb enough
to survive them?
